title: Found if block with identical else block
description:
`if-else` expressions with the same `if` and `else` parts are probably a result
of a copy-paste. The result of the expression is independent of the condition.

title: Found redundant wildcard patterns
description: Successive wildcard patterns (`_`) add noise to code, use the rest pattern (`..`) instead.

title: Class methods should utilize `this`
description: If a class method does not use `this`, it can sometimes be made into a static function. If you do convert the method into a static function, instances of the class that call that particular method have to be converted to a static call as well `(MyClass.callStaticMethod())`

title: Class methods should utilize `this`
description: If a class method does not use `this`, it can sometimes be made into a static function. If you do convert the method into a static function, instances of the class that call that particular method have to be converted to a static call as well `(MyClass.callStaticMethod())`

title: Detected usage of the `any` type
description: The `any` type can sometimes leak into your codebase. TypeScript compiler skips the type checking of the `any` typed variables, so it creates a potential safety hole, and source of bugs in your codebase. We recommend using `unknown` or `never` type variable.

title: Detected usage of the `any` type
description: The `any` type can sometimes leak into your codebase. TypeScript compiler skips the type checking of the `any` typed variables, so it creates a potential safety hole, and source of bugs in your codebase. We recommend using `unknown` or `never` type variable.

title: Detected usage of the `any` type
description: The `any` type can sometimes leak into your codebase. TypeScript compiler skips the type checking of the `any` typed variables, so it creates a potential safety hole, and source of bugs in your codebase. We recommend using `unknown` or `never` type variable.

title: Detected usage of the `any` type
description: The `any` type can sometimes leak into your codebase. TypeScript compiler skips the type checking of the `any` typed variables, so it creates a potential safety hole, and source of bugs in your codebase. We recommend using `unknown` or `never` type variable.

title: Detected usage of the `any` type
description: The `any` type can sometimes leak into your codebase. TypeScript compiler skips the type checking of the `any` typed variables, so it creates a potential safety hole, and source of bugs in your codebase. We recommend using `unknown` or `never` type variable.

title: Found single-character string literal pattern
description:
Certain `str` functions, such as `.split()` and `.find()` work on patterns that
accept both string literals as well as characters. When using such functions,
prefer `char`s over single-character string literals as they are more
performant.

title: Found single-character string literal pattern
description:
Certain `str` functions, such as `.split()` and `.find()` work on patterns that
accept both string literals as well as characters. When using such functions,
prefer `char`s over single-character string literals as they are more
performant.

title: Found single-character string literal pattern
description:

accept both string literals as well as characters. When using such functions,
prefer `char`s over single-character string literals as they are more
performant.

title: Found single-character string literal pattern
description:
Certain `str` functions, such as `.split()` and `.find()` work on patterns that
accept both string literals as well as characters. When using such functions,
prefer `char`s over single-character string literals as they are more
performant.

title: Use shorthand property syntax for object literals
description: ECMAScript 6 provides a concise form for defining object literal methods and properties. This syntax can make defining complex object literals much cleaner.

title: Use shorthand property syntax for object literals
description: ECMAScript 6 provides a concise form for defining object literal methods and properties. This syntax can make defining complex object literals much cleaner.

title: Use shorthand property syntax for object literals
description: ECMAScript 6 provides a concise form for defining object literal methods and properties. This syntax can make defining complex object literals much cleaner.

title: Function with cyclomatic complexity higher than threshold
description:
A function with high cyclomatic complexity can be hard to understand and
maintain. Cyclomatic complexity is a software metric that measures the number of
independent paths through a function. A higher cyclomatic complexity indicates
that the function has more decision points and is more complex.

title: `async function` should have `await` expression
description: A function that does not contain any `await` expressions should not be `async` (except for some edge cases in TypeScript which are discussed below). Asynchronous functions in JavaScript behave differently than other functions in two important ways:

title: Empty call to `new()`
description:
The `new()` function is used to initialise an object with specific data.
If no arguments are passed, the behaviour is identical to `default()`.

title: Empty call to `new()`
description:
The `new()` function is used to initialise an object with specific data.
If no arguments are passed, the behaviour is identical to `default()`.

title: Logical operator can be refactored to optional chain
description: The [optional chaining](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/Optional_chaining) operator can be used to perform null checks before accessing a property, or calling a function.

title: Logical operator can be refactored to optional chain
description: The [optional chaining](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/Optional_chaining) operator can be used to perform null checks before accessing a property, or calling a function.

title: Use `const` declarations for variables that are never reassigned
description: Variables that are never re-assigned a new value after their initial declaration should be declared with the `const` keyword. This prevents the programmer from erroneously re-assigning to a read-only variable, and informs those reading the code that a variable is a constant value.

title: Use `const` declarations for variables that are never reassigned
description: Variables that are never re-assigned a new value after their initial declaration should be declared with the `const` keyword. This prevents the programmer from erroneously re-assigning to a read-only variable, and informs those reading the code that a variable is a constant value.

title: Found use of `.clone()` in assignment
description:
Consider using `.clone_from()` instead of assigning the result of `.clone()`.

`clone_from()` may perform better in some cases because some structs may have custom implementations of `clone_from()`, which improve performance by avoiding needless allocations.

title: Found use of `.clone()` in assignment

title: Prefer not to declare variables in global scope
description: It is considered a best practice to avoid 'polluting' the global scope with variables that are intended to be local to the script. Global variables created from a script can produce name collisions with global variables created from another script, which will usually lead to runtime errors or unexpected behavior. It is mostly useful for browser scripts.

description: It is considered a best practice to avoid 'polluting' the global scope with variables that are intended to be local to the script. Global variables created from a script can produce name collisions with global variables created from another script, which will usually lead to runtime errors or unexpected behavior. It is mostly useful for browser scripts.

title: Function with cyclomatic complexity higher than threshold
description:
A function with high cyclomatic complexity can be hard to understand and
maintain. Cyclomatic complexity is a software metric that measures the number of
independent paths through a function. A higher cyclomatic complexity indicates
that the function has more decision points and is more complex.

title: Found non-null assertions
description: Using non-null assertions cancels out the benefits of strict null-checking, and introduces the possibility of runtime errors. Avoid non-null assertions unless absolutely necessary. If you still need to use one, write a [skipcq comment](https://docs.deepsource.com/docs/issues-ignore-rules) to explain why it is safe.

If you still need to use one, write a [skipcq comment](https://docs.deepsource.com/docs/issues-ignore-rules) to explain why it is safe.

title: No default cases in switch statements
description: Some code conventions require that all switch statements have a default case, even if the default case is empty. The idea is to always explicitly state what the default behavior should be so that it's clear that the developer didn't forget to handle the default fallback case.
